quiz_title: AL — Athlete Nutrition - Foundations of Sports Nutrition v2.0 [EN]

questions.text: Which macronutrient serves as the primary fuel source during high-intensity football training and matches?
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates are broken down into glucose and stored as glycogen in muscles and the liver. During high-intensity exercise, the body relies almost exclusively on glycogen as its fast energy source. Fat oxidation is too slow to sustain explosive efforts; protein provides only ~5% of energy even during prolonged exercise.

questions.text: How much fluid should a footballer drink in the 2–3 hours before a match to ensure optimal pre-match hydration?
questions.explanation: Sports nutrition guidelines (ACSM / UEFA) recommend 400–600 ml of fluid in the 2–3 hours before exercise to achieve adequate hydration without gastric discomfort. Drinking too little leaves the athlete in a mild dehydration state; drinking too much risks bloating and hyponatraemia.
distractor_pool: As much as possible until full
No fluid — drink only during the match

questions.text: When should a footballer consume their last substantial pre-match meal to allow adequate digestion before kick-off?
questions.explanation: Consuming a high-carbohydrate, moderate-protein, low-fat meal 3–4 hours before kick-off gives the body enough time to digest, absorb nutrients, and restore liver glycogen without causing GI discomfort during play. A small carbohydrate snack (e.g. banana) can follow 30–60 minutes before kick-off.
correct_variants: Around 3–4 hours prior to the game
distractor_pool: The night before only — no meal on match day

questions.text: Within what time window after a match should a footballer prioritise carbohydrate intake to maximise glycogen resynthesis?
questions.explanation: Muscle glycogen synthase activity is highest in the 30–60 minutes immediately post-exercise ('glycogen window'). Consuming 1–1.2 g of carbohydrate per kg body weight in this window accelerates resynthesis significantly compared to delaying intake by 2+ hours. Adding protein also enhances the response.
correct_variants: Within the first hour after the final whistle
distractor_pool: Timing does not matter — total daily carbs are all that counts
Only if the player feels hungry

questions.text: Which nutrient is primarily responsible for repairing and rebuilding muscle tissue after intense football training?
questions.explanation: Dietary protein provides amino acids that act as building blocks for muscle protein synthesis (MPS). After resistance or high-intensity training, MPS is elevated and protein intake (20–40 g per serving) stimulates repair and adaptation. Carbohydrates refuel glycogen but do not directly drive MPS; fats and micronutrients play supporting roles.
